questions.prompt: An SEO consultant cold-emails you, guarantees a #1 ranking in 30 days, and claims a 'special relationship' with Google. What's the right response?

choices.text: Walk away — every signal here is a textbook SEO scam pattern. No one can guarantee rankings, no one has a special relationship with Google, and unsolicited cold pitches are almost never legitimate.
Ask if they can also guarantee AI Overview placement.

questions.explanation: Each red flag alone is a deal-breaker; together they're certain fraud. From Google's own doc: 'No one can guarantee a #1 ranking on Google. Beware of SEOs that claim to guarantee rankings, allege a special relationship with Google, or advertise a priority submit to Google.' Walk away.

questions.prompt: An SEO firm proposes registering 30 'supporting' domains 'on your behalf' that will redirect to your site to boost authority. Should you proceed?

choices.text: Yes — more domains means more authority.

No. Shadow-domain schemes are listed by Google as a classic scam. The SEO retains ownership; if you part ways, they redirect those domains to a competitor (or worse). Also a likely spam-policy violation. Walk away — and always own your own domains.
Only if they're cheaper than $20 each.
Only if the domains have keywords in them.

questions.explanation: Google explicitly lists this as a known scam: 'One common scam is the creation of "shadow" domains... These shadow domains are often owned by the SEO who claims to be working on a client's behalf.' Beyond the spam-policy risk, the leverage dynamic is bad — they can rug-pull you. Never let an SEO register domains 'for you.'
